chapters.text_md: Horowitz candidly describes the psychological toll of near-failure, including layoffs and the constant fear of running out of money, arguing that facing it honestly is essential to leading through crisis.

chapters.title: Making the Tough Calls
chapters.text_md: The book offers concrete guidance on firing executives, conducting layoffs respectfully, and promoting managers, stressing that avoiding hard decisions only makes them harder later.

books.title: The E-Myth Revisited
books.author: Michael E. Gerber
books.description: Why most small businesses fail and how systems can fix that
insights: Most owners are technicians suffering an 'entrepreneurial seizure,' not true entrepreneurs.
Work ON your business, not just IN it, by building repeatable systems.
Model your business like a franchise prototype, even if you never franchise it.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: The E-Myth Revisited debunks the myth that most businesses are started by skilled entrepreneurs; in reality most are started by technicians. Gerber argues that systemizing the business is the key to sustainable growth.

chapters.title: The Entrepreneur, Manager, and Technician
chapters.text_md: Every owner contains three personalities: the visionary Entrepreneur, the orderly Manager, and the hands-on Technician. Most small-business owners are dominated by the Technician and get trapped working in the business.

chapters.title: The Franchise Prototype
chapters.text_md: Treat your business as if you were about to franchise it, building turnkey systems and documented processes so it can run without depending on any one person, including the owner.

books.title: Traction
books.author: Gino Wickman
books.description: A practical operating system to gain control of your business
insights: Strengthen Six Key Components: Vision, People, Data, Issues, Process, and Traction.
Quarterly 'Rocks' turn big goals into 90-day priorities everyone can execute.
Weekly Level 10 Meetings keep teams focused and solve issues fast.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Traction introduces the Entrepreneurial Operating System (EOS), a practical framework to help leadership teams gain control of their business by strengthening Six Key Components.

chapters.title: The Six Key Components
chapters.text_md: EOS organizes a business around Vision, People, Data, Issues, Process, and Traction. Strengthening all six creates a healthy, well-run company.

chapters.title: Rocks and the Level 10 Meeting
chapters.text_md: Set three to seven quarterly 'Rocks' to translate vision into action. The weekly 90-minute Level 10 Meeting reviews scorecards, rocks, and issues to keep the organization on track.

books.title: Blue Ocean Strategy
books.author: W. Chan Kim and Renée Mauborgne
books.description: How to create uncontested market space and make rivals irrelevant
insights: Compete in uncontested 'blue oceans' instead of bloody 'red ocean' markets.
Value innovation means pursuing differentiation and low cost simultaneously.
The Four Actions Framework (Eliminate-Reduce-Raise-Create) reshapes value curves.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Lasting success comes not from beating competitors in existing markets ('red oceans') but from creating new, uncontested market space ('blue oceans'), shown through cases like Cirque du Soleil.

chapters.title: Value Innovation
chapters.text_md: Rejecting the trade-off between value and cost, companies should create leaps in buyer value while reducing costs by eliminating factors an industry takes for granted, reshaping the strategy landscape.

chapters.title: The Four Actions Framework
chapters.text_md: Ask which factors to eliminate, reduce, raise, or create versus industry standards. Plotting these on a 'strategy canvas' reveals a new value curve that opens fresh market space.

books.title: The Innovator's Dilemma
books.author: Clayton M. Christensen
books.description: Why great companies fail by doing everything 'right'
insights: Sustaining innovations improve existing products; disruptive innovations create new markets from below.
Listening closely to current customers can blind managers to disruptive threats.
Established firms should spin off small units to pursue disruptive opportunities.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Christensen examines why successful, well-managed companies lose leadership when confronted by disruptive technologies, showing that the very practices that make firms excellent can cause them to miss disruption.

chapters.title: Sustaining vs. Disruptive Technologies
chapters.text_md: Sustaining technologies improve performance mainstream customers value; disruptive technologies initially underperform but are cheaper or simpler, appealing to new customers before overtaking the mainstream.

chapters.title: The Resource Allocation Trap
chapters.text_md: Rational resource allocation pushes firms toward profitable sustaining innovations and away from small disruptive markets. Christensen recommends autonomous units sized to profit in emerging markets.

books.title: Contagious
books.author: Jonah Berger
books.description: The science of why products, ideas, and behaviors catch on
insights: Word of mouth is driven by six STEPPS: Social Currency, Triggers, Emotion, Public, Practical Value, Stories.
Content that makes people look smart or sparks strong emotion gets shared.
Public visibility and triggers turn private choices into viral behaviors.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Berger explains why certain ideas and products spread through word of mouth while others are ignored, distilling years of research into six key drivers, the STEPPS framework.

chapters.title: The STEPPS Framework
chapters.text_md: Six ingredients of contagiousness: Social Currency, Triggers, Emotion, Public, Practical Value, and Stories, each illustrated with real marketing case studies.

chapters.title: Emotion and Triggers Drive Action
chapters.text_md: High-arousal emotions push people to share, while ordinary environmental triggers keep products and ideas 'top of mind, tip of tongue' long after initial exposure.

books.title: Influence
books.author: Robert B. Cialdini
books.description: The classic study of the six universal principles of persuasion
insights: Six weapons of influence: reciprocity, commitment/consistency, social proof, liking, authority, scarcity.
People use automatic mental shortcuts, making them vulnerable to compliance tactics.
Recognizing these principles helps you persuade ethically and resist manipulation.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Cialdini explores the psychology of why people say yes, identifying six core principles that drive automatic, often unconscious compliance, drawn from decades of research and undercover fieldwork.

chapters.title: The Six Principles of Persuasion
chapters.text_md: Reciprocity, commitment and consistency, social proof, liking, authority, and scarcity each reliably trigger compliance, whether we're aware of them or not.

chapters.title: Automatic Compliance
chapters.text_md: In a complex world we rely on 'click-whirr' mental shortcuts, which compliance professionals exploit. Awareness of the triggers is the primary defense against manipulation.

books.title: Predictably Irrational
books.author: Dan Ariely
books.description: The hidden, systematic forces that shape our everyday decisions
insights: Human decisions are irrational, but the irrationality is systematic and predictable.
Relativity and arbitrary anchors, not absolute value, shape what we pay.
'Free' and social norms trigger disproportionate, often counterproductive reactions.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Using behavioral economics experiments, Ariely shows people don't decide rationally but irrationally in consistent, predictable ways, which can help design better choices and policies.

chapters.title: Relativity and Anchoring
chapters.text_md: We rarely judge value in absolute terms, instead comparing options to each other or to arbitrary 'anchors' that skew our future willingness to pay, even when meaningless.

chapters.title: Zero Cost and Social vs. Market Norms
chapters.text_md: 'Free' is an emotional hot button causing irrational trade-offs, and mixing social norms with market norms can backfire, souring relationships once cash enters a social exchange.

books.title: The Paradox of Choice
books.author: Barry Schwartz
books.description: Why having too many options makes us more anxious, not happier
insights: Beyond a point, more options increase anxiety and decrease satisfaction.
'Maximizers' who seek the best option are less happy than 'satisficers'.
Limiting choices, using rules, and lowering expectations can boost well-being.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Schwartz argues that while some choice is good, the modern explosion of options has made people more anxious and less satisfied rather than more free.

chapters.title: Maximizers vs. Satisficers
chapters.text_md: Maximizers exhaustively search for the optimal choice; satisficers settle for good enough. Maximizers make objectively better choices but feel worse, due to regret and unrealized alternatives.

chapters.title: The Cost of Freedom
chapters.text_md: Too much choice triggers regret and rising expectations, leading to disappointment even with good outcomes. Restricting options, adopting rules, and practicing gratitude reduce the burden.

books.title: Flow
books.author: Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi
books.description: How full immersion in an activity creates optimal experience
insights: Flow is complete absorption where challenge and skill are perfectly matched.
Clear goals and immediate feedback help sustain flow and focused attention.
People with an 'autotelic' personality find activities rewarding for their own sake.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Csikszentmihalyi's research into 'optimal experience'—being fully absorbed and enjoying an activity for its own sake—argues that true happiness comes from stretching ourselves to meet worthwhile challenges.

chapters.title: The Conditions for Flow
chapters.text_md: Flow arises when task challenge matches skill level, with clear goals and immediate feedback. Too much challenge causes anxiety, too little causes boredom, so flow lives in a narrow balance.

chapters.title: The Autotelic Personality
chapters.text_md: 'Autotelic' individuals find flow almost anywhere by pursuing activities as intrinsically rewarding. Cultivating this mindset lets anyone bring more flow into everyday life and work.

books.title: Stumbling on Happiness
books.author: Daniel Gilbert
books.description: Why the brain is remarkably bad at predicting future happiness
insights: Imagination fills gaps about the future inaccurately, distorting happiness forecasts.
The 'impact bias' makes us overestimate the intensity and duration of future emotions.
Asking someone living an experience predicts our future feelings better than imagining it.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Gilbert explores 'affective forecasting'—predicting how future events will feel—and why these predictions are so often wrong, due to flaws in memory, perception, and imagination.

chapters.title: The Flaws of Imagination
chapters.text_md: Imagination fills missing details with present assumptions, glosses over how quickly we adapt, and is distorted by our current emotional state, all skewing our forecasts about what will make us happy.

chapters.title: The Impact Bias and Surrogation
chapters.text_md: We overestimate how intensely and how long events will affect us, underestimating our resilience. Relying on real-time reports of others already having the experience is surprisingly more accurate.

books.title: The Happiness Advantage
books.author: Shawn Achor
books.description: Positive psychology proves happiness fuels success, not the reverse
insights: Happiness precedes and fuels success, not just follows it.
The brain performs better when positive rather than neutral or negative.
Small habits like gratitude and exercise raise your happiness baseline.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Achor, a positive-psychology researcher, argues conventional wisdom has success and happiness backwards: happiness is a precursor to greater success, and offers seven principles for applying this.

chapters.title: The Fulcrum and the Lever
chapters.text_md: Our mindset is the fulcrum and our abilities the lever; shifting the fulcrum through a more positive mindset increases the lever's power, making success a product of mindset, not just effort.

chapters.title: The Tetris Effect
chapters.text_md: By training the brain to scan for positives and possibilities instead of problems, people become primed to notice opportunities for growth, compounding into greater resilience and success.

books.title: The Four Agreements
books.author: Don Miguel Ruiz
books.description: Toltec wisdom: four simple agreements for personal freedom
insights: Be impeccable with your word: speak with integrity.
Don't take anything personally: others' actions reflect their own reality.
Don't make assumptions: communicate clearly and ask questions.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Ruiz distills ancient Toltec wisdom into four powerful agreements that break self-limiting beliefs formed through social conditioning he calls 'domestication,' offering a path to personal freedom.

chapters.title: Be Impeccable with Your Word
chapters.text_md: The most important agreement urges honesty and avoiding self-directed negativity or gossip, since words shape both self-image and reality, and misusing language is a root cause of suffering.

chapters.title: Don't Take Anything Personally
chapters.text_md: Other people's opinions and actions are projections of their own reality, not truths about you. Internalizing this frees a person from needless emotional suffering.

books.title: The Untethered Soul
books.author: Michael A. Singer
books.description: A guide to inner freedom by releasing the voice in your head
insights: You are the consciousness observing your thoughts, not the thoughts themselves.
Inner disturbance arises from resisting reality, not from reality itself.
Releasing blocked emotional energy leads to lasting inner peace.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Singer explores consciousness, teaching readers to distinguish their true self from their inner voice and emotional reactions, and to release psychological blocks for spiritual growth and freedom.

chapters.title: The Voice Inside Your Head
chapters.text_md: The constant inner monologue is a habitual mental pattern separate from true awareness. Observing it rather than being controlled by it is the first step toward inner liberation.

chapters.title: Letting Go
chapters.text_md: Releasing energy blockages formed by resisted emotions, by surrendering resistance rather than avoiding it, is presented as the core spiritual discipline and the path to lasting freedom.

books.title: A New Earth
books.author: Eckhart Tolle
books.description: Awakening to your life's purpose by transcending the ego
insights: The ego is a false, thought-based sense of self that creates suffering.
Presence, being fully in the Now, dissolves egoic patterns.
Humanity is undergoing a collective shift toward higher consciousness.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Tolle expands on 'The Power of Now,' examining how the collective ego drives dysfunction in individuals and society, presenting present-moment awareness as the key to personal and collective transformation.

chapters.title: The Ego
chapters.text_md: The ego is an illusory identity built from thought, roles, and possessions that constantly seeks validation, causing conflict and suffering. Recognizing egoic patterns is the first step to freedom.

chapters.title: Finding Who You Truly Are
chapters.text_md: By practicing presence and observing the ego without judgment, readers access a deeper sense of being beyond thought, which Tolle describes as one's true, formless identity.

books.title: The Seven Spiritual Laws of Success
books.author: Deepak Chopra
books.description: Seven spiritual principles for turning dreams into reality
insights: Success flows from aligning with the field of pure potentiality.
Giving and receiving are part of the same universal flow of abundance.
Detachment from outcomes paradoxically enables their fulfillment.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Chopra presents seven spiritual laws drawn from natural law and ancient wisdom that he argues govern all creation, including true success, bringing abundance and fulfillment when applied consciously.

chapters.title: The Law of Pure Potentiality
chapters.text_md: Our essential nature is pure consciousness connected to infinite possibility, and practices like meditation reconnect us with that source of creativity and abundance.

chapters.title: The Law of Giving
chapters.text_md: The universe operates through dynamic exchange, so freely giving what we seek keeps abundance circulating. Generosity is framed as a practical spiritual practice.

books.title: Tuesdays with Morrie
books.author: Mitch Albom
books.description: A dying professor's final life lessons to his former student
insights: Accepting death honestly makes living more meaningful.
Relationships and love matter more than career or possessions.
Forgiveness of others and oneself brings lasting peace.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Mitch Albom reconnects with his beloved professor Morrie Schwartz, dying of ALS. Their weekly Tuesday visits become a final 'class' on living, love, work, and death.

chapters.title: On Death and Dying
chapters.text_md: Morrie models facing mortality with openness rather than denial, arguing that acknowledging death makes people live more fully and appreciate each day.

chapters.title: On Love and Relationships
chapters.text_md: Morrie emphasizes that love and human connection, not money or achievement, give life ultimate meaning, urging Mitch to build a life around relationships rather than ambition alone.

books.title: Big Magic
books.author: Elizabeth Gilbert
books.description: Living a creative life beyond fear, guided by curiosity
insights: Curiosity is a more sustainable creative guide than passion.
Ideas are living forces that seek willing collaborators.
Fear will always be present; creativity means acting alongside it anyway.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Gilbert argues everyone has the right to a creative life regardless of talent or outcome, encouraging readers to pursue curiosity over perfection and make peace with fear.

chapters.title: Fear and Creativity
chapters.text_md: Fear is a natural companion to creativity, not an enemy to defeat. The goal is to invite fear along without letting it drive, framing courage as creating in fear's presence.

chapters.title: Ideas as Living Entities
chapters.text_md: Ideas are external forces circulating for a willing human partner, and showing up consistently is how one earns 'big magic.' Failing to act on an idea may cause it to move on.

books.title: The War of Art
books.author: Steven Pressfield
books.description: Breaking through creative blocks by defeating Resistance
insights: Resistance is the universal force opposing any creative or growth-oriented act.
Turning pro means showing up and doing the work daily, regardless of mood.
Fear of Resistance is often a sign the work truly matters.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Pressfield identifies 'Resistance'—procrastination, self-doubt, fear—as the internal force that stops people from pursuing their creative calling, and how to overcome it by 'turning pro.'

chapters.title: Defining Resistance
chapters.text_md: Resistance takes many disguises, from procrastination to self-sabotage, and is strongest right before a breakthrough. Naming it is the first step to disarming it.

chapters.title: Turning Pro
chapters.text_md: The antidote is the mindset of a professional: showing up daily, treating the work seriously, and not waiting for inspiration. Amateurs are ruled by fear; professionals act despite it.

books.title: Bird by Bird
books.author: Anne Lamott
books.description: Instructions on writing and life, one small step at a time
insights: Write imperfect first drafts to get past perfectionism.
Break overwhelming tasks into small, manageable pieces, bird by bird.
Pay attention to small, specific details; that's where good writing lives.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Lamott offers candid, humorous advice on the craft of writing, blending practical technique with reflections on creativity, fear, and life. The title comes from advice to tackle a huge task 'bird by bird.'

chapters.title: Shitty First Drafts
chapters.text_md: All good writing begins with messy, imperfect first drafts, freeing writers from the paralyzing need to get it right immediately. Revision, not initial perfection, is where good writing happens.

chapters.title: Bird by Bird
chapters.text_md: Any daunting task becomes manageable when broken into small, immediate steps rather than tackled all at once, a philosophy that underlies her approach to both craft and anxiety.

books.title: Steal Like an Artist
books.author: Austin Kleon
books.description: Ten ideas about creativity for those who feel overwhelmed
insights: Nothing is original; all creative work builds on what came before.
Collect and remix influences rather than chasing pure originality.
Do good work and share it consistently; don't wait for permission.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Kleon presents ten principles on creativity, arguing all artists borrow and transform ideas from those before them, demystifying the creative process and encouraging embracing influence.

chapters.title: Steal Like an Artist
chapters.text_md: No work is wholly original; artists should openly study and 'steal' from many influences, transforming borrowed pieces into something new so no single influence is too obvious.

chapters.title: Share Your Work
chapters.text_md: Creators should document and share their process publicly rather than waiting for a finished masterpiece, building an audience and habit through consistent visibility.

books.title: Range
books.author: David Epstein
books.description: Why generalists triumph in a specialized world
insights: Late specialization and broad sampling often beat early hyper-specialization.
Range of experience enables analogical thinking that solves novel problems.
Match quality between a person and their work matters more than a head start.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Epstein argues that in a world prizing narrow specialization, generalists with diverse experiences often have the edge in complex domains, contrasting the early-specialization and late-sampling paths.

chapters.title: The Power of Sampling
chapters.text_md: Delayed specialization lets people test many fields before finding the best 'match quality' between skills and interests. Sampling periods correlate with long-term success and satisfaction.

chapters.title: Analogical Thinking and Outsiders
chapters.text_md: People with broad backgrounds transfer knowledge across domains through analogy, often solving problems specialists miss, as in breakthroughs from outsiders applying lessons from unrelated fields.

books.title: Peak
books.author: Anders Ericsson
books.description: Secrets from the new science of expertise
insights: Deliberate, feedback-driven practice, not just time on task, builds real expertise.
Mental representations let experts perceive and process information differently.
The '10,000-hour rule' oversimplifies; the structure and quality of practice matter most.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Ericsson, whose research inspired the '10,000-hour rule,' explains that raw talent matters less than the right kind of practice, introducing 'deliberate practice' as the engine of expert performance.

chapters.title: Deliberate vs. Naive Practice
chapters.text_md: Simply repeating a task for years doesn't create expertise. Deliberate practice requires specific goals, immediate feedback, and error correction, unlike naive practice, which plateaus quickly.

chapters.title: Mental Representations
chapters.text_md: Experts build rich mental models that let them recognize patterns and self-correct faster than novices, whether in chess, music, or sport, built through structured practice.

books.title: Make Time
books.author: Jake Knapp and John Zeratsky
books.description: How to focus on what matters every day
insights: Pick one daily 'Highlight' instead of juggling an endless to-do list.
Guard focus time by defaulting distracting apps and notifications off.
Physical energy from movement, sleep, and food directly fuels mental focus.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Two former Google designers offer a flexible daily system for reclaiming focus from the 'Busy Bandwagon' and 'Infinity Pools,' built on a four-step framework: Highlight, Laser, Energize, Reflect.

chapters.title: Highlight and Laser
chapters.text_md: Each day, choose one Highlight, then use Laser tactics like blocking distractions and time-boxing to protect focused time, avoiding rigid productivity systems.

chapters.title: Energize and Reflect
chapters.text_md: Sustained focus depends on physical energy, so the authors recommend exercise, real food, and less sitting, and a daily Reflect step to experiment with tactics and track what works.

books.title: Hyperfocus
books.author: Chris Bailey
books.description: How to be more productive in a world of distraction
insights: Attention management, not time management, is the real key to productivity.
Hyperfocus, single-tasking with intention, produces far better work than multitasking.
Deliberate mind-wandering, or scatterfocus, boosts creativity and planning.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Bailey explores how attention, not time, is our most limited resource, teaching two modes: hyperfocus (deep single-task concentration) and scatterfocus (deliberate mind-wandering).

chapters.title: Hyperfocus
chapters.text_md: Choosing one clear task, eliminating distractions, and fully committing attention creates a flow-like productive state, with concrete steps to make it repeatable.

chapters.title: Scatterfocus
chapters.text_md: Deliberately letting the mind wander, rather than filling idle moments with a phone, activates the brain's default mode network, generating insights and complementing hyperfocus.

books.title: Indistractable
books.author: Nir Eyal
books.description: How to control your attention and choose your life
insights: Distraction is often an escape from uncomfortable internal triggers, not just tech.
Scheduling your values via timeboxing turns intentions into traceable actions.
Precommitment pacts make it harder to give in to distraction in the moment.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Eyal teaches readers to control their own attention, arguing distraction is driven by internal discomfort as much as external triggers, offering a model to become 'indistractable.'

chapters.title: Mastering Internal Triggers
chapters.text_md: Most distraction starts with an uncomfortable internal state, boredom, loneliness, anxiety, that we try to escape. Identifying and reframing these triggers comes before reaching for a device.

chapters.title: Timeboxing and Pacts
chapters.text_md: Turning values into scheduled time closes the gap between intention and action, and making effort, price, or identity pacts prevents backsliding, alongside hacking back external triggers.

books.title: Hooked
books.author: Nir Eyal
books.description: How to build habit-forming products
insights: The Hook Model, trigger, action, variable reward, investment, drives habitual use.
Variable, unpredictable rewards are more engaging than fixed ones.
User investment in a product increases the likelihood they return.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Eyal explains the 'Hook Model,' a four-step cycle, trigger, action, variable reward, investment, that successful products use to create habitual engagement, with case studies from Instagram and Twitter.

chapters.title: The Hook Model
chapters.text_md: Each cycle begins with a trigger prompting an action, followed by a variable reward that keeps users curious. Unpredictable rewards are especially effective at driving repeat behavior.

chapters.title: Investment and Habit Formation
chapters.text_md: The investment phase asks users to put in data, effort, or social capital, increasing the odds they return. Repeating the cycle turns a conscious choice into an automatic habit.

books.title: Thinking in Bets
books.author: Annie Duke
books.description: Making smarter decisions when you don't have all the facts
insights: Judge decisions by the process, not just the outcome; 'resulting' is a trap.
Treat beliefs and choices as bets made under uncertainty, expressed as probabilities.
A truthseeking group of peers improves objectivity and calibration.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Former poker pro Annie Duke argues good decisions should be judged by process quality, not outcome, a fallacy she calls 'resulting,' teaching readers to think probabilistically and treat decisions as bets.

chapters.title: Resulting and Probabilistic Thinking
chapters.text_md: A good decision can produce a bad outcome due to luck, and vice versa. Duke urges evaluating the reasoning process and range of possible outcomes instead of just what happened.

chapters.title: Truthseeking Groups
chapters.text_md: Because we're prone to self-serving biases, Duke recommends 'truthseeking' groups that challenge your reasoning, and stating confidence as a percentage to improve calibration.

books.title: Superforecasting
books.author: Philip E. Tetlock
books.description: The art and science of prediction
insights: Precise, incremental probability estimates beat vague or overconfident predictions.
Regularly updating beliefs in small steps as evidence arrives improves accuracy.
Forecasting skill can be taught and depends on humility and open-mindedness.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Based on the Good Judgment Project, Tetlock studies 'superforecasters', ordinary people who consistently outperform experts at predicting world events, distilling the habits that make them accurate.

chapters.title: Breaking Predictions into Probabilities
chapters.text_md: Superforecasters avoid vague, binary predictions, assigning precise numerical probabilities and updating them incrementally as new evidence arrives, outperforming bold, overconfident forecasts.

chapters.title: Traits of Superforecasters
chapters.text_md: They share intellectual humility, active open-mindedness, comfort with numbers, and breaking big questions into smaller sub-questions. Forecasting is a learnable skill, not a fixed gift.

books.title: Factfulness
books.author: Hans Rosling
books.description: Ten instincts that distort how we see the world
insights: The world is generally better and improving faster than most people believe.
Ten deep-seated instincts systematically distort our worldview.
Checking claims against real data, not gut instinct, gives an accurate picture.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Global-health researcher Hans Rosling shows, using data, that most people dramatically misjudge the world as poorer and more dangerous than it is, identifying ten instinctive biases and how to counter them.

chapters.title: The Gap and Negativity Instincts
chapters.text_md: We instinctively split the world into two opposing categories when most people live in the middle, and we notice bad news more than steady improvement, both distorting our sense of progress.

chapters.title: The Straight Line Instinct
chapters.text_md: We assume trends continue in a straight line, ignoring that most trends curve or level off. Rosling urges looking for the actual shape of data rather than extrapolating blindly.

books.title: The Gifts of Imperfection
books.author: Brené Brown
books.description: Let go of who you think you're supposed to be
insights: Perfectionism is a shield against shame, not a path to growth.
Genuine belonging requires authenticity, including showing up as imperfect.
Shame loses power when it's spoken and met with self-compassion and connection.

chapters.title: Overview
chapters.text_md: Brené Brown draws on a decade studying shame and vulnerability to define 'wholehearted living', engaging with life from a place of worthiness rather than perfectionism, with ten guideposts.

chapters.title: Cultivating Authenticity
chapters.text_md: Brown distinguishes perfectionism, a shield against shame, from healthy striving, arguing perfectionism blocks growth and connection. Authenticity means choosing to be real over being liked.

chapters.title: Wholehearted Living
chapters.text_md: Wholehearted people believe they are worthy of love and belonging as they are, not because of achievement. Shame resilience, self-compassion, and reaching out is the path to embracing imperfection.
